Folding the Future: How AI is Reshaping Protein Engineering
===========================================================

Since the birth of biotechnology, researchers have been on a quest to develop better proteins: enzymes that retain their function at high temperatures, antibodies that bind targets with one-in-a-million specificity, or structural proteins that are stronger, lighter, and cheaper to make than traditional materials. Nature’s versatile biological machines, proteins, are composed of strings of amino acids that fold into complex, dynamic shapes—like origami. The function of the protein is determined by its structure, which, in turn, depends on the amino acid sequence.

The vision of engineering custom proteins with specified properties and functions by designing a specific amino acid sequence has captivated scientists for a long time. The problem is that protein sequence space is practically immeasurable, and trying to engineer a protein to perform a specific function is a Sisyphean task. For decades, protein engineers evolved functions—such as an enzyme’s thermostability or substrate affinity—in the lab using a method called directed evolution. Alternatively, they would attempt to rationally design the desired functions by looking at the protein structure (a 3D model obtained through X-ray crystallography or cryo-electron microscopy) and trying to figure out which protein regions they needed to change, followed by making hundreds of different mutated variants and testing them in the lab.

![Image 5](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a66aa220a55155318447ce/67e9a24fd66046f42e4ba6d0_AD_4nXdKjqEsQLb5hPIYlsqm1chGG3RK5r6nFdO6K8YwwVlg2Hq_eqgbINfwJSIA7fdFzYxx82HHaRXG9YB6kv5877GxeTa3BlbETnQ4MmckObAdCEU9aurzklqo44WoC4Y5MbmWt4Ga9w.jpeg)

_Protein structure visualizations help scientists understand how amino acid sequences dictate function—but until recently, designing these structures required laborious trial and error. [Emw/Wikimedia CC BY-SA 3.0]_

As artificial intelligence continues to transform protein engineering, thought leaders and startup pioneers alike will converge at this year’s [SynBioBeta: The Global Synthetic Biology Conference](https://www.synbiobeta.com/events/synbiobeta-2025) to discuss the latest breakthroughs in generative models for protein design. Presenters will highlight how these computational tools are fast becoming an essential driver of innovation across the synthetic biology landscape.

#### The AlphaFold Revolution

Given the arduous and inefficient nature of engineering proteins in the lab, the field was long overdue for a computational makeover. However, until about 2018, the computational power required to solve a protein structure was considered unattainable. Back in 1969, molecular biologist Cyrus Levinthal estimated that it would take longer than the age of the known universe to solve the protein folding problem since a typical-length protein can fold into 10 300 possible configurations. But that doesn’t mean no one has tried it.

Since the late 1980s, scientists developed molecular physics-based models to try to figure out the rules of protein folding. To compare how well the different models worked, researchers established the [Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction](https://predictioncenter.org/) (CASP) challenge, a protein structure prediction competition that began in 1994. In the course of the competition, teams would attempt to solve the structure of a protein based on their models and then compare those predictions with experimentally determined structures.

The majority of the initial models, such as[Rosetta](https://rosettacommons.org/software/) developed in 2024 Nobel Laurate [David Baker’s lab](https://www.bakerlab.org/) at the [University of Washington](https://www.washington.edu/), were physics-based. While these models could accurately describe the thermodynamic forces driving protein folding, they lacked the computational power to brute-force 10 300 possible configurations. The development of deep learning algorithms in the early 2010s was a breakthrough moment for protein structure prediction. In 2018, a team from [DeepMind](https://deepmind.google/), the UK artificial intelligence lab bought by Google in 2014, entered into the thirteenth CASP competition with a model called AlphaFold and[placed first](https://www.forbes.com/sites/samshead/2018/12/03/deepmind-starts-to-show-how-ai-can-be-used-to-solve-scientific-problems/). By the next competition in 2020, AlphaFold 2, a revamped algorithm from DeepMind, left all other models in the dust.

AlphaFold, which won the[2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2024/press-release/) for its researchers, was a true breakthrough in protein folding prediction. AlphaFold is a neural network-based model that predicts protein structures with atomic accuracy without requiring nearly as much computational power as physics-based models. To do that, the algorithm first searches genetic databases for similar protein sequences and creates a multiple-sequence alignment. Then, it generates a pairwise representation to encode spatial relationships between amino acids. The refined pairwise information is passed through a transformer network to produce a final prediction of the protein's structure.

![Image 6](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a66aa220a55155318447ce/67e9a24fdf229649cba030f2_AD_4nXdvqvK0WkbvuQtiwGkVJtuLxYYVvHRBMtTIQe_3cjM3-8m2AzleeWsMr_ZnOqvfQFKZffAbIUobJR0UIN7xsIMXyZrk6s5IaSCB-XZ-k_WAJeAx8pBoB6e6O1YfjtcBq8aLmEvSAQ.jpeg)

_AlphaFold, developed by DeepMind, revolutionized structural biology by using deep learning to predict protein structures with atomic-level precision. [DALL-E]_

This AI tool has revolutionized biology, enabling the design of new proteins for medicine, energy, and sustainability. Since 2020, AlphaFold has been used to predict the structures of millions of proteins, making waves across the scientific community. (As of the writing of this article, the[Nature paper](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-021-03819-2) describing AlphaFold has been cited 32998 times). The latest AlphaFold 3 model is[freely available](https://blog.google/technology/ai/google-deepmind-isomorphic-alphafold-3-ai-model/) for non-commercial research use, democratizing protein engineering—something we could not have even imagined a decade ago.

#### Beyond AlphaFold

Since the breakthrough of AlphaFold, other AI models have been developed by academic labs and biotech companies, including[EvoBind](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.07.23.501214v1),[ESMFold](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.ade2574), and[RFDiffusion](https://www.bakerlab.org/2023/07/11/diffusion-model-for-protein-design/), which can predict protein structure and function, as well as elucidate the folding of other molecule types (DNA and RNA) and model complex molecular interactions between proteins and their ligands.

[RFdiffusion](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06415-8) is a model built on an open-source algorithm called[RoseTTAFold](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.abj8754), developed by David Baker’s lab. It generates new protein structures in a manner similar to how DALL-E or Midjourney generate art. This tool was able to solve new design challenges, including molecular binding and oligomer design. Both RoseTTAFold and RFdiffusion have since then been upgraded to[All-Atom](https://www.ipd.uw.edu/2023/10/introducing-rosettafold-and-rfdiffusion-all-atom/) versions, which enable modeling of not just proteins but biological complexes made from different molecules, including DNA, RNA, small molecules, metals, and other bonded atoms.

As large language models like ChatGPT took the world by storm, scientists realized that you can use them for proteins as well—and this has turned out to be a very successful strategy. These gigantic models trained on extensive volumes of data enable scientists to generate de novo designs based not on protein structure but function. Dubbed[protein language models](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-86519-5) (PLMs), these tools are a lot more accessible for researchers because tuning a natural language model is much easier than developing conventional machine learning algorithms.

The progress in generative AI for protein design is by no means finished. Researchers are still working to refine the algorithms to create better proteins for specific applications, as well as expand the capabilities of what is possible for biology in the age of AI. Last month, NVIDIA released[GenMol](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/evaluating-genmol-as-a-generalist-foundation-model-for-molecular-generation/), a foundational model for molecular generation. GenMol offers a versatile molecular generative framework based on discrete diffusion and non-autoregressive decoding designed to streamline drug discovery from molecule design to lead optimization.

#### Generative Design in Synthetic Biology

So, what does this mean for synthetic biology and the bioeconomy?

![Image 7](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a66aa220a55155318447ce/67e9a24f144fa0a0da6eaf22_AD_4nXdjCqioii8K6IvLrLWPT0kl45eELwnO7RQ2yVWvJeRvIiOJEb0KrIHbX0oY5v8BLdZWs8qoDIbZS3qVEyV7-bCccEUx0Ocr8BOhGzqQEoadYFi6KoPGHnzTheO7i7ohO8WP7V3DXw.jpeg)

_AI-designed enzymes are already being used to improve industrial processes—from high-heat manufacturing to plastic degradation—boosting both performance and sustainability. [DALL-E]_

The dream of being able to design custom proteins has finally come true. For example, we can make new enzymes that are stable at high temperatures. Using enzymes that do not degrade in high-temperature industrial processes (such as making paper from wood pulp) translates into millions of dollars in savings. AI is also revolutionizing drug discovery research by allowing scientists to model how proteins and small-molecule drugs interact with their targets. Additionally, we can engineer proteins that have no equivalents in nature—which could help us solve the challenges of the twenty-first century.

Companies such as[Cradle](https://www.cradle.bio/) have been using protein language models to design better[enzymes](https://www.cradle.bio/blog/case-study-combining-ai-generated-mutations-with-human-expertise-for-better-enzyme-designs),[receptor-binding proteins](https://www.cradle.bio/blog/adaptyv-protein-design-competition), and[antibodies](https://www.cradle.bio/blog/antibody-ngs-data-initiative), with applications across the entire biotechnology spectrum, from industrial and food biotech to biopharma.[Absci](https://www.absci.com/) is transforming the biologics discovery pipeline by enabling[de novo](https://www.synbiobeta.com/read/absci-and-amd-team-up-to-revolutionize-drug-discovery-with-ai) antibody design. And [Ginkgo’s](https://www.ginkgo.bio/) models have been able to improve[PETase activity](https://foundrytheory.substack.com/p/the-petase-mask-fill-challenge), an enzyme capable of degrading plastic.

As the recent [Advanced Biotech for Sustainability (AB4S](https://www.ab4s.org/report))[report](https://www.ab4s.org/report) highlights, advanced biotechnology could reduce global emissions by 5% and generate $1 trillion in economic value across the food, agriculture, chemicals, personal care, and transportation fuels sectors. With generative AI becoming a mainstay in biotechnology, transforming the world through synthetic biology is no longer a pipe dream—it is just a work in progress.

[RFdiffusion](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06415-8) is a model built on an open-source algorithm called[RoseTTAFold](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.abj8754), developed by David Baker’s lab. It generates new protein structures in a manner similar to how DALL-E or Midjourney generate art. This tool was able to solve new design challenges, including molecular binding and oligomer design. Both RoseTTAFold and RFdiffusion have since then been upgraded to[All-Atom](https://www.ipd.uw.edu/2023/10/introducing-rosettafold-and-rfdiffusion-all-atom/) versions, which enable modeling of not just proteins but biological complexes made from different molecules, including DNA, RNA, small molecules, metals, and other bonded atoms.

As large language models like ChatGPT took the world by storm, scientists realized that you can use them for proteins as well—and this has turned out to be a very successful strategy. These gigantic models trained on extensive volumes of data enable scientists to generate de novo designs based not on protein structure but function. Dubbed[protein language models](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-86519-5) (PLMs), these tools are a lot more accessible for researchers because tuning a natural language model is much easier than developing conventional machine learning algorithms.

The progress in generative AI for protein design is by no means finished. Researchers are still working to refine the algorithms to create better proteins for specific applications, as well as expand the capabilities of what is possible for biology in the age of AI. Last month, NVIDIA released[GenMol](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/evaluating-genmol-as-a-generalist-foundation-model-for-molecular-generation/), a foundational model for molecular generation. GenMol offers a versatile molecular generative framework based on discrete diffusion and non-autoregressive decoding designed to streamline drug discovery from molecule design to lead optimization.

#### Generative Design in Synthetic Biology

So, what does this mean for synthetic biology and the bioeconomy?

_AI-designed enzymes are already being used to improve industrial processes—from high-heat manufacturing to plastic degradation—boosting both performance and sustainability. [DALL-E]_

The dream of being able to design custom proteins has finally come true. For example, we can make new enzymes that are stable at high temperatures. Using enzymes that do not degrade in high-temperature industrial processes (such as making paper from wood pulp) translates into millions of dollars in savings. # AI could accelerate protein engineering – key for developing new medicines

An AI approach developed by researchers from the University of Sheffield and AstraZeneca, could make it easier to design proteins needed for new treatments.

![An illustration of protein molecules](//cdn.sheffield.ac.uk/sites/default/files/styles/mobile_single_column_1x/public/2025-06/GettyImages-1402266493.jpg?h=cf144a73&itok=vwp_TCX-)

**An AI approach developed by researchers from the University of Sheffield and AstraZeneca, could make it easier to design proteins needed for new treatments.**

In a study published in the journal *Nature Machine Intelligence*, Sheffield computer scientists in collaboration with AstraZeneca and the University of Southampton have developed a new machine learning framework that has shown the potential to be more accurate at inverse protein folding than existing state-of-the-art methods.

Inverse protein folding is a critical process for creating novel proteins. It is the process of identifying amino acid sequences, the building blocks of proteins, that fold into a desired 3D protein structure and enable the protein to perform specific functions. Protein engineering plays a critical role in drug development by designing proteins that can bind to specific targets in the body. However, the process is challenging due to the complexity of protein folding and the difficulty in predicting how amino acid sequences will interact to form functional structures.

Scientists have turned to machine learning to more accurately predict which amino acid sequences will fold into stable, functional protein structures. These models are trained on large datasets of known protein sequences and structures to improve inverse folding predictions.

The new machine learning framework, called MapDiff, from the University of Sheffield, AstraZeneca and the University of Southampton, outperformed the most state-of-the-art AI in making successful predictions in simulated tests. The results are a promising basis to develop the technology further, which, if successful, could accelerate the design of the key proteins needed to develop new vaccines and gene therapies, and other therapeutic modalities. It also complements other recent advances, such as AlphaFold, which predicts a protein’s 3D structure by reversing the approach, starting with the protein fold and retrieving the potential amino acid sequences.

**Haiping Lu, Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Sheffield and the corresponding author of the study, said:**“This work represents a significant step forward in using AI to design proteins with desired structures. By learning how to generate amino acid sequences that are likely to fold into specific 3D structures, our method opens new possibilities for designing new therapeutic proteins, which can be used in various therapeutic applications. It’s exciting to see AI helping us tackle such a fundamental challenge in biology.”

**Peizhen Bai, Senior Machine Learning Scientist at AstraZeneca, who developed the AI as part of his PhD at the University of Sheffield’s School of Computer Science, said:** “During my PhD, I was motivated by the potential of AI to accelerate biological discovery. I’m proud that our method, MapDiff, helps design protein sequences that are more likely to fold into desired 3D structures — a key step towards advancing next-generation therapeutics.”

The study was a result of a non-funded collaboration of combined industry expertise, and builds on previous work between the Sheffield computer scientists and AstraZeneca, which developed [DrugBAN](https://www.sheffield.ac.uk/news/ai-could-speed-discovery-new-medicines) – AI that can predict whether a candidate drug will bind with its intended target protein molecules inside the human body and could accelerate the discovery of new medicines. The paper became one of the most cited papers from the journal *Nature Machine Intelligence* in 2023.

The latest paper, *Mask-prior-guided denoising diffusion improves inverse protein folding*, is published in *Nature Machine Intelligence*. [Read the paper in full.](https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-025-01042-6)

# Turbocharging Protein Engineering with AI

October 9, 2024 • by
Marc Airhart and Anita Shiva

Biotech advances from UT’s new Deep Proteins group are changing the game with help from artificial intelligence.

Researchers study the three dimensional structures of molecules on a wall-sized video display at the TACC Visualization Lab.

---

[*Listen to the audio version of this story*](https://utexas.box.com/s/knjxdkcl9yb3aim1xew5fq03q6969iov)

Working as a chemist in Houston, Danny Diaz spent a lot of time plodding his way through crosstown traffic, pondering how to speed up his research.

“I realized that my impact in the short term would be limited to the amount of chemistry experiments I could do with my hands,” he recalled.

It occurred to him that with software engineering and data analytics, he could run virtual experiments any time, even while he slept or was traveling. And the results could help him bypass some of the trial and error that’s common in the lab.

When he took this mindset with him into a chemistry graduate program at The University of Texas at Austin, he arrived at a starting line of sorts along with other researchers around the world who were just beginning to develop ambitious new approaches to designing proteins for a host of useful applications ranging from therapeutics to agrochemicals to food additives to enzymes that break down plastic waste. Among the most promising applications for protein engineering are therapeutics to treat diseases such as cancer, autoimmune disorders, rare metabolic disorders, Alzheimer’s, infectious diseases and more. Because the traditional way of developing proteins into therapeutics is slow, expensive and riddled with failure, it was a field primed for disruption.

In 2020, when Diaz was a second-year Ph.D. student, Google’s DeepMind team released the second version of [AlphaFold](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AlphaFold), a powerful artificial intelligence tool for predicting what three-dimensional shape any given protein would take, based on its sequence of amino acids. Predicting these protein shapes, called structures, had been a grand challenge in biology for decades and, although the model’s predictions weren’t perfect, further refinements have brought it so close that the problem is now considered more or less solved.

The key to AlphaFold’s success is a new paradigm for building AI models, called the transformer architecture, which is also the foundation of generative AI tools like ChatGPT. While most folks know it as a way to generate useful text, researchers in biology and chemistry quickly realized that transformer models could also be applied to designing protein-based biotechnologies. AlphaFold has enabled many of Diaz’s research projects, where he uses it to train AI models with protein structures where experimental data doesn’t exist.

**Planning for Proteins**

You might recall from high school biology that proteins are made up of amino acids strung together like beads on a necklace. The exact order of the amino acids, or the protein sequence, determines the 3D shape it will take and how it will function. However, there are more potential variations of protein sequences than stars in the universe, with most of these variations being unstable or non-functional. This makes testing them in the lab to find improved, highly functional proteins extremely arduous.

To make a protein useful for applications in health and medicine often requires introducing variations, via genetic engineering, so that a protein can be used in a vaccine, as a cancer drug or to biomanufacture goods. Identifying mutations that optimize proteins is difficult, also, because it traditionally relies on scientists leveraging scarce data in the scientific literature related to any specific protein to guide their intuition.

Enter artificial intelligence. The dream is that AI models can learn from a wide range of data sources and then guide researchers to better designs faster. Professor Andrew Ellington in the Department of Molecular Biosciences, for example, enlisted Diaz early on in a project to explore leveraging machine learning to speed up the painstaking process of protein engineering. That project, known as [MutCompute](https://mutcompute.com/), was an early example of how AI can narrow the search space to tweak proteins and make them more stable, easier to produce or more accurate at hitting targets. The resulting AI model helped enable a [breakthrough in plastic-eating enzymes](https://molecularbiosci.utexas.edu/news/research/plastic-eating-enzyme-could-eliminate-billions-tons-landfill-waste).

Fast forward to today, and Diaz, now a research scientist in the Department of Computer Science at UT Austin, co-leads the [Deep Proteins](https://ml.utexas.edu/deep-proteins) group, a team based in UT Austin’s [Institute for Foundations of Machine Learning](https://www.ifml.institute/) (IFML), which is funded by the National Science Foundation. Alongside the IFML director and Deep Proteins co-leader Adam Klivans, Diaz and other researchers collaborate with experimental scientists who produce and test AI-designed proteins in labs across UT as well as at partner institutions, including Houston Methodist Research Institute, the University of Minnesota and Cornell University.

“One of the things that makes UT special in this space is that we connect world-class AI researchers with world-class scientists in biochemistry to accelerate biotechnology development, all under one roof,” Klivans said. “Deep Proteins, which develops foundational protein AI tools with an eye towards real-world applications, was designed to be interdisciplinary from the start.”

Deep Proteins researchers draw on lessons from many disciplines, for example, to develop an AI technique called EvoRank that trains an AI model to [learn from nature’s track record of protein evolution](https://cns.utexas.edu/news/research/ai-trained-evolutions-playbook-develops-proteins-spur-drug-and-scientific-discovery) in order to predict evolutionarily plausible novel mutations.

Diaz realized that even if he didn’t have high quality mutational data from experiments in the lab, he could observe the diversity of proteins produced by nature.

“We wondered, can we learn the underlying rules nature used to produce this diversity?” he said. “Once these rules are learned by an AI model, we will be able to filter out mutations that kill proteins on the computer so we can focus on those that don’t in the lab, thus accelerating our ability to develop new protein-based biotechnologies.”

**Protein Superpowers**

To make commercially viable drugs, one of the superpowers researchers need to endow proteins with is the ability to be produced in high quantities, quickly and affordably. Working with professors Andrew Ellington, Jessie Zhang and others, Deep Proteins researchers used an AI model to do just that for an Alzheimer’s drug that is traditionally derived from daffodils, a costly and time-consuming process that is vulnerable to fickle growing conditions. They updated the model they had used earlier for the plastic-eating enzyme, extending it to the regions in proteins that interact with small molecules and vitamins.

“Proteins do not exist in isolation,” Diaz said. “They exist in a soup mixed with other proteins, RNA, DNA, vitamins, minerals, fats, sugars and other small molecules. Therefore, it’s critical that the AI is trained to understand not just protein chemistry but all of biochemistry.”

Guided by this updated AI model, [MutComputeX](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46356-y), they [engineered *E. coli* bacteria to produce a precursor to the Alzheimer’s drug galantamine](https://cns.utexas.edu/news/research/alzheimers-drug-fermented-help-ai-and-bacteria-moves-closer-reality), allowing it to be grown in a lab more cheaply and reliably.

Boosting yield is also a key goal in a collaboration between the Deep Proteins group and research associate professor Everett Stone’s lab. That effort is seeking a treatment for a form of breast cancer that is highly dependent on a key metabolite called serine. Starting with a human enzyme that naturally breaks down serine, the researchers used their AI models to develop a more stable version that has seven times higher yield than the natural version. This version, which they’ve filed a patent for, is now in animal trials.

Another useful superpower is for a protein to bind more tightly to a target, a feature known as binding affinity. This generally means it will be more effective at a given task, such as when an antibody binds to a protein on the surface of a virus and prevents it from infecting cells. In a collaboration with Jimmy Gollihar at Houston Methodist Research Institute, Diaz used MutComputeX to dramatically increase the binding affinity of two antibodies against the virus that causes COVID-19.

Yet another protein superpower that’s especially useful for vaccines is stability, the knack for keeping its shape in the face of temperature or chemical changes experienced during the manufacturing and shipping process and once injected into your body.

Some viruses have proteins on their surfaces that allow them to infect cells and that are good at evading the immune system because they change shape. To generate the best immune response, vaccines based on these viral proteins have to lock them in the shape they typically take before infecting a cell. That was the key advance made by UT Austin professor of molecular biosciences Jason McLellan for the [COVID-19 vaccines](https://molecularbiosci.utexas.edu/news/research/covid-19-vaccines-ut-ties-arrived-quickly-after-years-making) and [RSV vaccines](https://cns.utexas.edu/news/features/fda-approved-rsv-vaccine-enabled-work-ut-molecular-biologist). McLellan’s lab is now working with the Deep Proteins group to explore how AI might help speed up the design process for future vaccines for other viruses, including as a part of a major new [multi-million-dollar project led by La Jolla Institute for Immunology](https://www.lji.org/news-events/news/post/lji-launches-americas-shield-to-develop-life-saving-herpesvirus-vaccines/).

“We would normally spend a lot of time manually staring at structures, identifying amino acid substitutions, and maybe our hit rate wasn’t particularly high, like we’d have to try 100 substitutions to find 20 that worked,” said McLellan, one of the world’s leading vaccine innovators. “I think with machine learning, it’s going to have a higher hit rate eventually. Maybe we’ll get the same 20 substitutions, but only have to test 40.”

**Predicting Stabilizing Mutations**

Before developing the evolution-inspired technique EvoRank, Diaz, Klivans and Jeffrey Ouyang Zhang, a fourth-year computer science doctoral student, used their combined expertise to develop one particularly elegant algorithm, [Mutate Everything](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/f03cb785864596fa5901f1359d23fd81-Paper-Conference.pdf). Starting from either AlphaFold or ESM, another AI model that predicts a protein’s structure based on its amino acid sequence, Mutate Everything can predict protein stability for single and higher-order mutations, with the aim of significantly accelerating research and development of protein-based biotechnologies. Building off their framework, Stability Oracle, which was recently [described in a Nature Communications paper](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-49780-2), the AI worked to identify and recommend single mutations for protein stabilization.

Mutate Everythingwill be retrained and updated as more protein experimental data becomes available, which makes the model important for applications in industries that rely on biotechnology, such as in the development of life-saving medications. The research team has made the algorithm publicly available to increase translation to industry use.

“Our computational tools can tell them how to stabilize a protein, which can save them a lot of time, headache and money,” Ouyang-Zhang said.

Still, AI researchers also must cope with having only limited experimental data on the properties of proteins to adequately train AI models to understand how proteins work and thus how to engineer them to work differently. That’s why newer efforts, like the Deep Proteins group’s work to learn from natural evolution via EvoRank, can complement the models that are based on experimental data.

> Healthcare costs [could] decimate our country and put significant financial burdens on future generations. AI-engineered proteins can be the superheroes we need.”
>
> Danny Diaz

Diaz is now working to launch a biotech startup company focused on inventing novel AI-inspired proteins to address obesity, diabetes and cancer.

“Nearly half of Americans are prediabetic or diabetic, and about as many will be diagnosed with cancer at some point in their life,” Diaz said. “Healthcare spending is currently outpacing GDP growth and currently nearly 20% of GDP. If we continue, these healthcare costs will decimate our country and put significant financial burdens on future generations. AI-engineered proteins can be the superheroes we need.”

Six years ago, when Diaz was getting started, the field of AI-guided protein engineering was brand new. Now the Deep Proteins team is among the most established and respected players in this space at a university anywhere. They also have a track record of AI-designed proteins to point to, with models that more traditional labs increasingly can benefit from.

“It’s hard to discover new things in established fields,” Diaz said. “With AI for protein engineering, there are lots of opportunities to do really cool, innovative work and that can change the world.”

*Update: On October 9, the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded for computational protein design and protein structure prediction, including by experts involved in the creation of AlphaFold.*

Highlights
----------

*   •AI applications are vital for protein structure prediction, PPIs, and molecular modeling. 
*   •AI tools enable analysis of biological sequences, RNA structure prediction, and CRISPR-based gene editing techniques. 
*   •AI holds vast promise for personalized medicine, biomarker discovery, drug interaction, omics analysis, and toxicity studies. 

Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have revolutionized pharmaceutical research, particularly in protein and nucleic acid studies. This review summarizes the current status of AI and ML applications in the pharmaceutical sector, focusing on innovative tools, web servers, and databases. This paper highlights how these technologies address key challenges in drug development including high costs, lengthy timelines, and the complexity of biological systems. Furthermore, the potential of AI in personalized medicine, cancer drug response prediction, and biomarker identification is discussed. The integration of AI and ML in pharmaceutical research promises to accelerate drug discovery, reduce development costs, and ultimately lead to more effective and personalized therapeutic strategies.

Introduction
------------

Proteins play versatile roles in human biology due to their involvement in metabolic processes. Protein engineering, on the other hand, focuses on modifying the phenotype of living organisms, enhancing antibody efficacy, and improving enzyme catalysis. These concepts are further illustrated in [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#f0005).[(p1)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0005) For better drug discovery, enzyme development, and innovations in biotechnological products, there is a need to understand the protein structure–function relationship. Traditional methods, such as X-ray crystallography and nuclear magnetic resonance, are used to determine three-dimensional (3D) protein structures. However, these methods are slow, expensive, and only work for certain proteins.[(p2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0010) Additionally, they struggle with large-scale analyses of proteins and RNA, making them less suitable for high-throughput studies.[(p3)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0015)

Figure 1. Summary of the intervention of AI and ML approaches in protein research includes a need of protein engineering for innovative healthcare products. It drives into the requirement of AI and ML in protein research, protein language models, and crucial factors involved in protein research. It also includes current challenges in AI/ML methods and future perspectives covering ethical and legal issues, regulatory compliance, and unbiased data interpretation. Crucial factors of protein research include protein structure determination, protein function, and analysis of interaction with DNA and RNA. Protein purification and expression with high purity and yield are important for structural and functional bodies. Stability and protein folding, and posttranslational modifications such as phosphorylation and glycosylation affect protein function. The challenges in AI applications are related to data quality, availability, data privacy, security, and data labeling. The overfitting, underfitting, and bias in AI models are crucial for accurate interpretation and results outcomes (created using [Biorender.com](http://biorender.com/)).

It is important to study protein–protein and protein–nucleic acid interactions involving disordered linkers, as well as dynamic complexes. Disordered linkers connecting protein domains are essential for identifying specific DNA sequences. Disordered proteins can mediate RNA sequence specificity, while intraprotein linkers can influence the binding properties of RNA-binding proteins. The length and flexibility of linkers or loops connecting different protein domains can significantly impact allosteric regulation, particularly when dealing with weakly interacting partners. Tethering proteins can improve protein complex solubility, while DNA cross-linking can be induced through the intrinsically disordered region.[(p4)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0020)

Traditional methods for the determination of protein structures, such as X-ray crystallography and nuclear magnetic resonance, are time-consuming and expensive. Artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled AlphaFold developed by DeepMind can help predict the 3D protein structures of proteins from amino acid sequences with atomic-level accuracy.[(p5)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0025) Tools like RoseTTAFold and OpenFold enhance structure prediction and protein design, while AI-driven resources such as the Protein Data Bank and UniProt support protein modeling and structural analysis. The dysfunctional interactions among cells, proteins, and networks that contribute to diseases require an understanding of protein–protein interactions (PPIs).[(p6)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0030) AI-based tools such as DDMut-PPI can help predict the effects of mutations on PPIs through graph-based deep learning (DL). SEMA 2.0, a computational tool designed to predict B-cell conformational epitopes, is crucial for the development of antibodies and vaccines, whereas AI-assisted molecular docking tools, such as LightDockServer, help with the efficient modeling of protein–ligand interactions. The AI algorithm aids in analyzing vast biological datasets, enabling quick drug target identification and development. For example, it facilitates G protein studies through tools like GproteinDb, ArrestingDb, and the Biased Signaling Atlas.[(p7)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0035)

AI databases, such as FireProtDB, can assist in enzyme engineering by assessing protein thermostability. AI-powered models, such as DeepCRISPR, CRISTA, and DeepHE, can predict the optimal guide RNAs (gRNAs) for precise genome editing. AI can help identify anti-CRISPR proteins for the regulatory control of gene editing and predict RNA modifications, such as methylation, that affect disease mechanisms and treatment efficiency using tools like MODOMICS. AI has revolutionized personalized medicine by analyzing patient-specific genomic and proteomic data. For example, AI-driven databases like CREAMMIST predict cancer drug responses based on pharmacogenomics data. AI has revolutionized drug discovery by improving efficiency and cost reduction through tools such as TOXRIC for the evaluation of drug safety, AggMapNet for data analysis in drug discovery, and ACFIS2.0 for enhanced fragment-based drug discovery for conditions such as Parkinson’s disease and cancer. AI tools provide assistance with bioinformatics research through automated data analysis and visualization, such as GeoBind, which is used for the identification of nucleic acid binding interfaces on proteins, and the DoriC 12.0 database, which is used for prokaryotic genome replication studies.[(p8)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0040)

AI and machine learning (ML) enable continuous process verification with ease due to their advanced contextualization capabilities. Multiple systems implemented by the pharmaceutical industry churn a large amount of data; thus, it can be challenging for pharmaceutical manufacturers to cope with process verification reporting. AI and ML can be used to accurately predict various resources for a drug to forecast the best schedule for pharmaceutical product manufacturing with minimal waste.

AI can help identify potential issues in raw materials, such as impurities or incorrect composition, before these materials enter the production line. This ensures quality control and regulatory compliance. Internet of Things in collaboration with AI helps manufacturers identify products and packaging types and monitor the condition of machinery in real time. Despite the immense potential of AI in the pharmaceutical and drug development industry, AI remains complementary to human expertise. Human oversight, validation, and regulatory compliance remain critical issues due to the black box operation of AI for patient safety along with ethical standards.[(p9)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0045)

When utilizing Generative AI in the pharmaceutical industry, safeguarding sensitive patients along with research information is critical. The data security policy requires strict adherence to practices such as data encryption, access control, and secure data management to prevent data breaches. The introduction of AI tools into the pharmaceutical sector accelerates the identification of potential drug candidates and optimizes molecular design. Many of the AI-powered tools and databases discussed here are freely available as web servers or open access databases. The democratization of advanced research tools has the potential to accelerate innovation across the global scientific community.

This review highlights the growing integration of computer science, biology, and pharmaceuticals. This interdisciplinary approach fosters novel solutions to complex biological problems. AI can harbor biases or inaccuracies due to incomplete or skewed datasets. It is mandatory to continuously refine these training datasets with high-quality and updated data to enhance their accuracy and reliability.[(p10)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0050),[(p11)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0055)

AI and nucleic acid research
----------------------------

The study of RNA biology includes the chemical composition, structure, and interaction of RNA with proteins. These interactions play a collective role in pathogenesis and disease outcomes. RNA plays critical roles in key biological processes, including transcription, translation, gene regulation, and telomere maintenance. It is also widely used in bioengineering, including mRNA vaccines and biomachines.[(p12)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0060) Predicting RNA structure, including how RNA bases pair and fold into 3D shapes, is essential for understanding its molecular functions, stability, and interactions with proteins.[(p13)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0065) Chemical modifications to mRNA can influence gene processing, mRNA stability, and protein production. These changes are critical for understanding diseases, cell functions, and organism growth.[(p14)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0070) Additionally, the interactions between protein complexes and RNA determine RNA’s fate and function. By identifying and characterizing these interactions, researchers can explore critical biological processes such as mRNA translation and localization, as well as their role in human diseases.[(p15)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0075)

The DNA mutation may have an impact on mRNA splicing, resulting in aberrant splicing and disease condition due to functional alterations. Thus, there is a need to identify the mutations responsible for alteration of splicing along with the prediction of their functional impact for interpretation of the mutation of unknown function for the betterment of human therapeutics.[(p16)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0080) The interaction of thousands of RNA molecules within cells and tissues plays a crucial role in determining both cellular functions and the overall characteristics of organisms.[(p17)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0085)

Targeting RNAs with small molecules can address emerging medical needs. More in-depth research is required for the identification of RNA targets, screening of chemical compound libraries, and lead optimization.[(p18)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0090) But despite the growing interest in the scientific community, RNA and therapeutic protein-targeted drug discovery are still in the nascent stages. AI tools help researchers understand how RNA and proteins interact with small molecules. Many pharmaceutical companies currently use AI to accelerate drug discovery targeting RNA and proteins.[(p19)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0095)

The interventions of AI, ML, and computer vision can enhance many aspects of human visual perception for the identification of meaningful clinical patterns. AI/ML enables faster diagnosis and tracking of disease progression by applying pattern recognition and segmentation techniques to medical images. The generative algorithms for computational augmentation can be used for existing clinical and imaging data sets along with new predictive models like DL.[(p20)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0100) AI in drug development can effectively analyze the huge amounts of data and can provide better solutions depending on the learned datasets. Research on small molecules for better products along with customer satisfaction involves economical drug preparation methods and efficacious and stable products.[(p21)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0105)

AI and ML are also essential for the study of antigen–antibody interactions. The specific, along with discontinuous binding sites on the antigen structure that are further recognized by the antibodies generated by the B cells, are called conformational B-cell epitopes. Prediction of conformational B-cell epitopes in antigens is essential for vaccine development and immunology. These predictions can be done by AI-based large pretrained protein language models, such as spatial modeling with AI (SEMA). The AI model has studied the spatial arrangements of the selected antigen target along with its primary sequence for effective prediction of B-cell confirmational epitopes in consideration of the conformational and multimeric states of antigens.[(p22)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0110) The structural characterization of disordered linkers and dynamic complexes, including protein–protein and protein–nucleic acid interactions, can be performed using AI-enabled tools such as AlphaFold and Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS).[(p23)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0115)

Proteins are made from mRNA through a process called translation. This process is tightly controlled and plays a key role in developing mRNA-based vaccines and treatments. The methods for RNA purification along with sequencing library preparation have limitations of resultant data disconnection from the absolute mRNA reference and require a more accurate assessment tool for protein biosynthesis to calculate absolute translational rates. Many methods have been used for RNA design such as RNAinverse, including adaptive random walk, stochastic local search, seeding heuristics, genetic algorithms, constraint programming, simulated annealing, and hierarchical decomposition. The prediction of folding sequences with some models of energy along with the design of sequences of bases for targeted secondary structures are cumbersome and can be addressed by the intervention of DL through reinforcement learning.[(p12)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0060) AI-based tools use structured regions and code optimality of the mRNA for designing more efficient mRNA.[(p24)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0120)

Optimizing the production of metabolites and pharmaceuticals through biosynthetic engineering requires regulating gene expression. Promoters are used to initiate gene transcription and control the flow of metabolic reactions in pathways. Native promoters have limited continuous strength and poor regulatory scope. The redesigned cells for the creation of gene circuits and synthetic biological systems require engineered promoters for the regulation of gene expression. DL tools can be used for the manipulation of eukaryotic and prokaryotic promoter elements through analysis of distribution patterns inherent in native promoter sequences, such as generative adversarial networks. This tool uses trained promoter sequences and differential RNA sequencing for the development of synthetic promoters.[(p25)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0125)

AI and protein research
-----------------------

Protein design involves a specific 3D protein structure that is required for precise atomic interactions. The protein structure is encoded by sequence elements known as the protein alphabet. Amino acid residues are vital for protein function, whereas the size of the amino acid alphabet is related to the complexity and computational hurdles in protein analysis. The natural alphabet is difficult to interpret into protein function inference, and it has an impact on the graphical representation of proteins. There is a need to analyze the link between protein sequence and structure to uncover the function of the complex protein. The AI-enabled tool RaacFold, which is a web-based sequence and structure viewer, can be used to decrease protein complexity and provide better perspectives of biomacromolecule interaction.[(p26)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0130)

The time it takes to analyze proteins depends on the available data. To obtain accurate protein sequences, researchers must carefully train models using neural networks that consider protein building blocks. DL-based methods can be used to limit expensive protein database searches by accurately predicting protein features.[(p27)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0135) AI-based tools are used to study the structure and function of G protein signaling, which play a key role in cellular and physiological responses. These tools help develop new therapeutics, such as ligands and inhibitors, targeting cancer mutants like G protein. AI-enabled GproteinDb, ArretsingDb, and Biased Signaling Atlas can be used for G protein studies investigating G protein interface interactions and predicting selectivity determinants enabling mutagenesis experiments. This can be helpful for better studying the autoregulation of signaling, allosteric modulation, binding sites, G protein selectivity, and signaling.[(p28)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0140)

Many efforts have been made to explore the uncharted space of membrane protein structures using topological data. Transmembrane proteins are vital for living cells for cellular communication, signaling, maintenance of cell structure, energy production, and molecular transport across membranes. The hydrophobic nature of the same is responsible for issues in structure determination, which can be addressed by AI-based tools such as AlphaFold2 and UniTmp web resources by using the Protein Data Bank of Transmembrane Protein, a topology data bank of transmembrane proteins for innovations such as the human transmembrane proteome.[(p29)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0145)

Coding and non-coding RNA classification are complex processes due to their overlapping functions and characteristics, and are required for protein creation for essential cellular functions. Many classification tools involving RNA descriptors with intervention of ML algorithms have been developed from nucleotide and amino acid sequences, similarity searches from protein databases, and reference genomes.[(p30)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0150)

Computational tools are designed for _in silico_ evaluation to reduce the time and resources involved in identifying the design paradigm for proteins required for computational algorithms for predictable design of DNA nanostructures such as Magic DNA, Oxview, and V-helix. These tools are not functional for the 3D visualization of nanostructures, including free-hand manipulations. Along with the underlying data models, they are not proficient for the _in silico_ evaluation of design using molecular dynamic simulations and involve limited nucleic acids. Because of the biological instability of RNA-based catalysts, proteins are the preferred choice for enzymatic reactions in the development of functionalized nanostructures. Refining structure prediction tools by AI will help in the design of recombinant and fusion proteins, such as the online modeling environment by Comprehensive Alternative Transcript Atlas based on geNe Annotation (CATANA).[(p31)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0155)

RNA modifications in living organisms are induced post-transcriptionally or co-transcriptionally with the help of enzymes, resulting in hypermodified residues. RNA molecules can be modified from external sources such as the environment. These RNA modifications are important for RNA metabolism, transport, localization, stability, and transability, and involve interactions with proteins as well as ribonucleoproteins. Intentional RNA modifications through artificial chemical modifications can be performed for therapeutic purposes. AI and ML can be used for RNA modifications for the detection and quantification of modified residues, such as in the MODOMICS database. It provides information related to the chemical structures of modified ribonucleosides and their biosynthetic pathways. It also provides information on the location of modified residues in RNA sequences as well as RNA-modifying enzymes.[(p32)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1359644625000753#b0160)

This review focuses on the application of AI and ML to structural biology through the development of atomic-level models and web servers to assess protein structure predictions, interactions, and stability. There is a need to develop models for the effective prediction of protein structures based on amino acid sequences, proper _in silico_ evaluations, and better computational design support for the study of recombinant proteins and functionalized DNA nanostructures. Abstract
--------

Artificial intelligence (AI) has revolutionized the protein engineering process from multiple aspects, including representing protein information, generating protein designs, and evaluating protein properties. This review aims to introduce the recent progress of AI in protein research. We first introduce how AI models represent protein sequences, structures, and other properties. Further, the applications of generative models in protein design are introduced. The use of predictive models and smart agents in the evaluation process is then discussed, including high-precision protein property simulation and wet lab experimental design. Additionally, we discuss the future development of AI in protein research and the potential challenges it may encounter.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent breakthroughs like AlphaFold and EvoBind enhance protein prediction accuracy, streamlining design processes and facilitating innovations in therapeutic and industrial applications.

enriched_points.point_title: Integration of AI with CRISPR technology
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI-driven frameworks optimize CRISPR gene editing, improving target specificity and efficiency while enabling rapid prototyping of novel therapeutic proteins for disease treatment.
Emerging AI tools enable dynamic, real-time protein design adjustments, accelerating experimentation and enhancing adaptability in response to experimental outcomes and novel challenges.
